mnemonics.title: Memory Read Sequence
mnemonics.content: READ OPERATION SEQUENCE: ADDRESS GOES TO MAR, THEN MAR TO BUS, READ SIGNAL SET, WAIT FOR DATA, DATA TO MDR, MDR TO REGISTER - REMEMBER: A B R W D M R (Address Bus Read Wait Data Memory Register)

mnemonics.title: Stack Operations
mnemonics.content: PUSH = POINTS DOWN BEFORE PUT (decrement SP, then store). POP = GET BEFORE UP (retrieve data, then increment SP) - REMEMBER: PUSH DOWN, POP UP

mnemonics.title: Endianness
mnemonics.content: LITTLE-ENDIAN = LEAST SIGNIFICANT BYTE AT LOWEST ADDRESS. BIG-ENDIAN = BIG ADDRESS AT START. REMEMBER: LITTLE = LOW ADDRESS GETS LITTLE (LEAST SIGNIFICANT) BYTE

mnemonics.title: Memory Hierarchy Speed
mnemonics.content: REGISTER (1 CYCLE) < CACHE (FEW CYCLES) < RAM (HUNDREDS) < DISK (MILLIONS). REMEMBER: F A S T - Fastest Access Slower Tiers

acronyms.usage: Holds the address of memory location to be accessed during read/write operations
Temporarily holds data being transferred between CPU and memory
Holds address of next instruction to be fetched from memory
Points to current top of stack, used implicitly by PUSH/POP instructions
Allows devices to transfer data to/from memory without CPU intervention
Signal used to access a column of data in DRAM memory
Signal used to access a row of data in DRAM memory

visualTips.visualization: Draw a bus diagram with CPU on left, memory on right. Show address bus going from MAR to memory, data bus returning from memory to MDR. Label READ signal arrow going to memory.

Draw memory as a vertical column with high addresses at top. Show SP pointing to current top. For PUSH: draw SP arrow moving DOWN (to lower address) then data being written. For POP: show data being read then SP arrow moving UP.
For 0x12345678 at address 0x100: Draw 4 boxes. Little-endian: 78 at 0x100, 56 at 0x101, 34 at 0x102, 12 at 0x103. Big-endian: opposite - 12 at 0x100.
Draw an inverted pyramid. Top (smallest, fastest): Registers. Next layer: Cache. Next: Main Memory (RAM). Bottom (largest, slowest): Secondary Storage (Disk/SSD). Label each with access time.

analogies.analogy: Like a house address you write down before going to the mailbox to get or send mail - you need the address before you can access anything.
Like a stack of plates in a cafeteria - you can only add (PUSH) or remove (POP) from the top. You can't directly access plates in the middle without removing ones on top.
Like going to a library for a specific book. If it's on your desk (cache hit), you get it instantly. If you need to go to the stacks (cache miss), it takes much longer.
Like hiring a delivery service to move furniture while you continue working. Without DMA (doing it yourself), you stop working to move items. With DMA, you and the delivery work in parallel.
Like a checkpoint that ensures all cars (memory operations) ahead of it have crossed before allowing cars behind to proceed, preventing traffic (data) confusion.
